coredata.dc:description: Single component and one precursor based white-light carbon dots (SO-WCDs) from solvent-free technique are tremendous potential in white light LED(WLED) lighting. However, no relating work is reported up to now. Using N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) as only precursor, we achieved, for the first time, the SO-WCDs by the solvent-free technique. Our SO-WCDs exhibit impressively fluorescent/phosphorescent dual emission and warm (in solid state)/cold (in liquid state) dual mode white light. The warm white light has a low correlated color temperature (CCT = 3765 K) and high color rendering index (CRI = 90.6), making it suitable for natural light-like WLEDs. Based on control experiments and instrumental analysis, it is proposed that the warm white light originated from synergistical contribution of the inherent fluorescence from carbon cores, the red shifted fluorescence from aggregation-induced effect (AIR) and the phosphorescence from carbon cores, while the cold white light from the inherent fluorescence from carbon cores and the red shifted fluorescence from AIR. Our work has provided a new solution for cold/warm white lighting production and further promoted the rapid and large-scale industrial preparation of the single-component white CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single component and one precursor based white-light carbon dots (SO-WCDs) from solvent-free technique are tremendous potential in white light LED(WLED) lighting. However, no relating work is reported up to now. Using N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) as only precursor, we achieved, for the first time, the SO-WCDs by the solvent-free technique. Our SO-WCDs exhibit impressively fluorescent/phosphorescent dual emission and warm (in solid state)/cold (in liquid state) dual mode white light. The warm white light has a low correlated color temperature (CCT = 3765 K) and high color rendering index (CRI = 90.6), making it suitable for natural light-like WLEDs. Based on control experiments and instrumental analysis, it is proposed that the warm white light originated from synergistical contribution of the inherent fluorescence from carbon cores, the red shifted fluorescence from aggregation-induced effect (AIR) and the phosphorescence from carbon cores, while the cold white light from the inherent fluorescence from carbon cores and the red shifted fluorescence from AIR. Our work has provided a new solution for cold/warm white lighting production and further promoted the rapid and large-scale industrial preparation of the single-component white CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: According to statistics, lighting consumes 15–20 % global electricity and contributes 5 % global greenhouse gas. For this reason, WLEDs are being developed in various countries [1–3] because of its high energy efficiency and long work life. The current WLEDs are of multi-component architecture, namely a blue LED chip (e.g., InGaN) integrating with a complementary color (e.g., yellow) phosphor or a blue LED chip combing with R, G, and B phosphors, for example, Lin et al.’ work [4]. The technique has shortcomings in manufacturing, miniaturization, color adequacy and stability, and chemical composition control and homogeneousness [5–7]. As a result, single-component white emission WLED has an urgent need. Rare earth-based phosphors, metal-organic complexes and organic molecules have been reported as such material [8]. But rare earths and organometallics are costly, toxic, and scarce, whereas organic molecules hard to synthesize, short-lived, and chemically unstable. Consequently, there is a strong desire for new low-toxicity, easy to synthesize, environmentally compatible single-component white phosphors [9–15].

Carbon dots (CDs) are chemically stable, environmentally friendly, easy to synthesize, and possess rich optical properties, making them extensively used in sensing, imaging, latent fingerprint (LFP) detection, and illumination [16–21]. Apparently, CDs are ideal for preparing single-component white phosphors, namely single-component white CDs (sWCDs), as Yang et al. [22] and Wang et al.’s [23] works. For implementing sWCDs, constructing multiple emission is essential. There are usually two routes for achieving multiple emission and thus sWCDs. The one, introducing multiple fluorescence emission modes, including energy resonant transfer (ET), charge transfer (CT), aggregation-induced redshift (AIR) or emission (AIE), to expand fluorescence emission [23]. For example, Peng et al. prepared white carbon dots from 2,3-diaminopyridine solution by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Their white light were realized by the synergistic contribution of multi-fluorescence emission [18]. sWCDs with similar mechanism was also reported by Paul et al. [24], Lin et al. [25]. Usually, sWCDs based on the multiple fluorescent emission had a high CRI and low color temperature, facilitating WLEDs realization [18]. However, the self-absorption in multiple fluorescence emission limits energy efficiency. Consequently, researchers are more concerned about the other route for sWCDs, namely, fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-emission for sWCDs. The route can utilize efficiently 3/4 of the electrically generated triplet excitons as well as 1/4 of the singlet excitons for photoemission [22], as a result, it has higher energy efficiency, lower heat dissipation. It is also theoretically possible to achieve 100 % quantum efficiency by utilizing triplet and singlet excitons together. In addition, it also has larger Stokes shift favoring white light output. The high effectiveness of the latter has been demonstrated by the carbon nitride quantum dos reported by Yang et al. [22]. The route was also be used to achieve the carbon dot with white afterglow by Bai et al. [26] and is being improved [23,27].

However, sWCDs with fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-emission reported were associated with multi precursors and wet chemistry methods, such as solvothermal and hydrothermal method. The wet chemistry synthesis needs intricated post-processing, extra solvent consumption and doping operation relating with solvent, which poses heterogeneities and complexities in CD's microscopic structures and composition, worsening by multiple precursors [17,18,28]. Moreover, the wet chemistry methods are far from CDs industrialization manufacturing [26]. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop sWCDs based on single precursor solid phase method.

The report presents single-component white-light carbon dots (SO-WCDs) synthesized via a one-precursor, solvent-free method. It explains their white lighting mechanism involving both fluorescence and phosphorescence. Additionally, SO-WCDs emit warm white light in solid form and cold white light in liquid form (when dissolved in water), offering varied color temperatures and CRI within a single CD material. Our work enhances the convenience and versatility of CD applications and optical modulation.

N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), ethanol and hydrochloric acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. All chemicals and solvents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

SO-WCDs were synthesized in one step by solid phase method using MBA as a single precursor. As shown in Scheme 1 , 500 mg of MBA and 0.1 ml of hydrochloric acid (as a catalyst, dehydrating agent and initiator) were transferred in a stainless-steel reactor with a PTFE lining. The reactor was then heated to 180 °C and kept at that temperature for 10 h. The solid product in the reactor was dialyzed in deionized water for 12 h to purify CDs (Dialysis Membranes with a MWCO of 8000). The water was replaced every 3 h. Subsequently, the purified CDs were freeze-dried, collected and stored at room temperature to obtain SO-WCDs (SO-WCDs(l) are obtained by placing SO-WCDs in water, while the solid state of SO-WCDs is denoted as SO-WCDs(s)).

First, 10 mg of SO-WCDs was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water. Then, 2 g of PVA was slowly added to the water with 1 h stirring. The mixed solution converted into a viscous mixture. Next, the mixture was cooled to room temperature. Finally, white light films (denoted as P-CDs) were obtained. The P-CDs were covered onto the surface of a UV-LED lampshade to produce cold white WLEDs (cWLED). 10 mg of SO-WCDs was thoroughly milled and coated directly onto a UV-LED lampshade, which was excited by the UV-LED to produce warm white WLEDs (wWLED). The voltage used to excite the WLED spectrum is 3.2 V, the current is 300 mA, the ambient temperature is 25.3 °C and the ambient humidity is 65 % Relative Humidity (RH).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEOL JEM-2100F) was used to record the morphologies of CDs. XRD spectra of SO-WCDs were recorded by instrumentation (X'Pert PRO MPD). Raman spectra of SO-WCDs were recorded by Raman spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Dxi). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Semeron Escalab 250Xi) was used to analyze the chemical composition of CDs. Solid-phase phosphorescence was recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments FLS 1000). Analyzing pyrolysis processes was conducted by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA)(The heating rate was 10 °C/min from room temperature to 800 °C under nitrogen atmosphere). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurements were performed on a TA model DSC. Scanning was performed under nitrogen at a rate of 10 °C/min. The weight of the DSC sample was approximately 5 mg to avoid thermal gradients within the sample. Viscosity analysis was done by a rheometer (HAAKE MARS60). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements were executed by a Nicolet 5700 Infrared Spectrometer. UV absorption spectra of CD solutions were recorded by a PerkinElmer (Lambda 35). Solid/solution phase fluorescence were recorded by a Hitachi F-7000 Fluorescence Spectrometer and a PerkinElmer (LS 55), respectively. Solid-phase/liquid-phase fluorescence at 77 K was recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000), and the liquid nitrogen unit was used with an Oxford instrument (OptistatDN).

For verifying the single-component nature of our SO-WCDs, we purified them using Silica Gel column Chromatography (SGC) for 4 days. During this process, a mixture of dichloromethane (DCM) and methanol (MeOH) was used as the solvent, with the ratio of DCM to MeOH varying from 100:1 to 1:1. After the SGC separation, no sub-components were isolated, and all elution fractions, as well as the residues in the SGC, exhibited white emission. (Scheme 2, Supporting Information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies, structure and composition of the SO-WCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images show that the SO-WCDs were well dispersed (Fig. 1 ). The geometry of the SO-WCDs (circled by the red circles in Fig. 1) is quasi-spherical, and typical SO-WCDs. It has a particle size distribution of 2–6 nm, with an average size of 3.48 nm (Fig. 1a). Clear lattice fringes are visible in HRTEM images, with the red-circled area in Fig. 1b showing an enlarged view of the lattice planes. The d-spacing between the fringes is 0.23 nm, corresponding to (101) lattice spacing of graphene crystal structures. In the SAED image we observe regular hexagonal patterns, also indicating that SO-WCDs have a graphene crystal structure (Fig. 1c). The facts above mentioned suggests that the SO-WCDs have graphene-like crystalline/s in carbon cores, meaning that sp2 cardon (sp2 C) species and π-conjugated structural domains exist in the SO-WCDs.

To further prove our deduction, we recorded the XRD and Raman spectra of SO-WCDs (Fig. 2 ). The XRD diffraction peaks of SO-WCDs (Fig. 2a), located at ∼22° and ∼41°, correspond to the (002) and (101) planes of graphitic carbon in the CDs [16]. The peak, ∼22°, is strong and clear, meaning that our SO-WCDs have lots of graphene-like structures in carbon cores. The fact is also confirmed by the Raman spectra. The two peaks in the Raman spectrum, 1372 cm−1 and 1576 cm−1, correspond to the D-band (from disorder or defects) and the G-band (from the sp2 carbon network in the graphene-like structure) of the CDs [29], respectively, and the IG/ID equal to 0.96, indicating highly graphene-like structures (Fig. 2b).

The infrared spectral profiles of the SO-WCDs in solid (SO-WCDs(s)) and liquid (SO-WCDs(l)) states are shown in Fig. 3 (The solvent for the SO-WCDs(l) is water), manifesting that the SO-WCDs have plenty of surface chemical groups such as-OH, –COOH, –CO and NH2. The peak at 3504 cm−1 in SO-WCDs(s) and the 3554 at cm−1 in SO-WCDs(l) are the stretching vibration of the –OH species, and the peak at 3311 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of –NH2, the peak at 3064 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of –CH, the peak at 1647 cm−1 is the stretching vibrational absorption of CO, respectively. Among these functional groups, –OH species are new comparing with the precursor, MBA, meaning oxidation occurs during the CDs synthesis. In addition, the –OH peak in the SO-WCDs(s) is broad, strong and red-shifted (Δδ = 50 cm−1) when referred to that of the SO-WCDs(l). Similar situations have been reported, and it is possible that intensive hydrogen bonding exists in the SO-WCDs(s) [30–32].

XPS was used to further explore chemical composition of the SO-WCDs. The XPS full spectra revealed that the SO-WCDs mainly contained C, N and O species, associating with three characteristic peaks at 285.8 eV, 400.8 eV and 534.8 eV, respectively. The chemical constitution of the CDs is 68.86 % C, 19.86 % N and 11.28 % O, respectively, from the XPS data (Fig. 4 a). The SO-WCDs have higher C, less O and almost identified N, comparing with the precursor (MBA, with 54.49 % C, 18.16 % N and 20.76 % O). The observation indicates that dehydration condensation occurred during SO-WCDs synthesizing and N self-doping is presented in the SO-WCDs. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum shows four peaks at 284.2 eV, 285.8 eV, 287.9 eV, and 288.8 eV, corresponding to CC/C–C (50.1 % of the C1s spectrum), C–N (34.5 %), C–O (11.6 %), and CO (3.6 %), respectively. Among them, CC/C–C accounts for the highest percentage, meaning that a large amount of sp2 carbons exist in the SO-WCDs, namely lots of graphene-like structures in the carbon cores of the SO-WCDs. While the CO and C–O peaks indicate that the SO-WCDs have –COOH, –COO– species originated from the oxidation from MBA (Fig. 4b). The high-resolution N 1s spectrum exhibits three peaks at 399.2 eV, 400.3 eV, and 401.2 eV, corresponding to C–N–C (43.0 %), N–C3(32.4 %), and –NH2(24.6 %), respectively. N–C3 is graphitic nitrogen and of electron delocalization, being conducive to fluorescence emission of the SO-WCDs (Fig. 4c). The high-resolution O 1s spectrum shows two peaks at 531.2 eV and 532.2 eV, corresponding to CO (68.9 %), C–O (31.1 %), respectively. It indicates that the surface of SO-WCDs contained –COOH and –OH (Fig. 4d). In short, the XPS data are consistent with FTIR observations, proving that the SO-WCDs have a different surface state from carbon core. From the HRTEM, XPS and FTIR records, we can conclude that the SO-WCDs are indeed carbon dots, which hold conjugated structures composing of sp2 carbon and graphitic nitrogen.

Furthermore, the thermal and rheological behaviors of the SO-WCDs were investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), DSC and rheometry (Figs. S1–S3, Supporting Information), respectively. The SO-WCDs showed a single thermal decomposition (red curve in Fig. S1, Supporting Information) mode, being similar to the thermal decomposition of typical nitrogen-containing polymers (e.g., polyamides). The decomposition temperature was around 220 °C, far lower than the decomposition temperature of the carbon dots with higher carbon content. The observation revealed that there may be polymer chains or networks in the SO-WCDs [33], and it is demonstrated further by derivative thermogravimetry (DTG, blue curve in Fig. S1, Supporting Information). DTG indicates the rate of weight loss, the peaks in the DTG curve are used to estimate the rate of thermal degradation. The degradation peak (stage) at 370 °C corresponds to the decomposition of functional groups (amides, hydroxyls, unsaturated double bonds, etc.) on the polymer chains of the SO-WCDs. The second degradation peak (stage) occurred at 408 °C and corresponded mainly to the breakage of the polymer chains from the SO-WCDs. The third stage of degradation from 421 °C to 600 °C described the pyrolysis of the internal crosslinked polymer networks. The final residue was 13 wt%. The DTG behaviors were also similar to thermal degradation of polyamides. The glass transition temperature (Tg) of the SO-WCDs were measured by DSC. After the thermal histories had been removed, the endothermal and exothermal curves of the SO-WCDs were recorded in DSC. As shown in (Fig. S2 Supporting Information), both the endothermal and exothermal curves presented that the SO-WCDs had a Tg of 75 °C (similar to that of nylon 66), further supporting that the SO-WCDs were polymeric [34]. The viscosity change of the SO-WCDs was studied by a rheometer. As shown in (Fig. S3 Supporting Information), the SO-WCDs showed typical shear thinning behavior at 25 °C, a classic polymer rheological characteristic. In the meanwhile, the viscosity of the SO-WCDs decreased with increasing temperature in the range of 20–60 °C (Fig. S4, Supporting Information). Based on these experimental of TGA, DTG, DSC and rheometry, we can infer that the SO-WCDs are carbon polymeric dots in essence. And we can also deduce that the SO-WCDs consist of carbon cores and polymeric shells, which are conducive to solid state/aggregation luminescence and afterglow, such as AIR and phosphorescence.

The SO-WCDs showed impressively multiple luminescent phenomena (“multiple in one”), including warm white light at solid phase (SO-WCDs(s)), and cold white light at liquid phase (SO-WCDs(l)). “Multiple in one” in optical properties is of significance to application and pure studies. When UV exciting at 365 nm, SO-WCDs(s) emitted warm white fluorescence with peak center at 580 nm (Fig. 5 a). There was no significant change in the emission of SO-WCDs(s) as the excitation wavelength was changed, which implied weak excitation wavelength dependence (Fig. 5b). The white fluorescence of the SO-WCDs(s) is a Gaussian shaped wide distribution across visible (400–800) nm, with 200 nm of FWHM. The wide emission is favorable for achieving white light(Fig. 5b) [35]. The broad spectra obtained at different excitation wavelengths(320 nm, 340 nm and 365 nm) are classified into six colors (violet, blue, green, yellow, orange and red) from 380 nm (violet) to 700 nm (red). The spectral component ratio of violet-red intensity to total intensity is estimated from the FL data and is shown in the stack column. The spectral component ratio of blue-red intensity is about 95 % (Fig. 5c). The UV–Vis spectrum of SO-WCDs shows a broad absorption (ranging from 300-800, centered at 360 nm), which includes a weak sharp absorption peak at 240 nm, attributed to the π-π∗ transition of sp2 C species. The broad peak corresponds to the n-π∗ of other sub-fluorophores (such as CO, CN) in carbon cores (Fig. 5d). SO-WCDs(l) emitted cold white fluorescence centered at 435 nm under optimal excitation at 365 nm (Fig. 5e). When the excitation wavelength was changed, the SO-WCDs(l) clearly showed a certain excitation wavelength dependence (Fig. 5f). Why do SO-WCDs(l) exhibit wavelength dependence in fluorescence, while SO-WCDs(s) do not? The likely reason is that in SO-WCDs(l), CD aggregates of varying sizes are formed. These aggregates exhibit different degrees of redshift fluorescence, corresponding to different excitation wavelengths. As a result, SO-WCDs(l) display wavelength dependence. In contrast, the aggregates in the solid state have a relatively narrower size distribution, which means that SO-WCDs(s) do not exhibit excitation wavelength dependence.

((a) Physical photographs of fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of SO-WCDs(s) and warm white light of SO-WCDs(s); (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of SO-WCDs(s) at different excitation wavelengths; (c)The spectral compositions estimated from the fluorescence data of SO-WCDs(s), at different excitation wavelengths; (d) UV–Vis spectra of SO-WCDs; (e) Physical photographs of fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of SO-WCDs(l) and cool white light of SO-WCDs(l); (f) Fluorescence emission spectra of SO-SO-WCDs(l) at different excitation wavelengths.)

When UV-excitation closed, SO-WCDs(s) presented orange phosphorescence centered at 600 nm, lasting for 3 s (Fig. 6 a,b,6d, and a related video, Video S1, Supporting Information, demonstrated the phosphorescence of SO-WCDs(s)). The phosphorescence lasting time can be improved, such as applying Wang et al. [36]'s passivation technique. We passivated the SO-WCDs by PVA (Polyvinyl Alcohol) (P-CDs), suppressing non-radiative transitions of the CDs, and prolonged the phosphorescence from 3 s to 4 s (Video S2. Phosphorescent demonstration video of P-CDs, Supporting Information). The phosphorescence center peaks are red-shifted by 20 nm compared to the warm white fluorescence of SO-WCDs(s) (Fig. 5a vs. Fig. 6a). The phosphorescence decay spectra of the SO-WCDs under 365 nm excitation can be fitted to a triple exponential function. The life-time components were 2.70 (25.37 %), 18.84 (42.40 %), 112.79 (32.23 %) ms, respectively, and the average lifetime is 45 ms (Fig. 6c). The results indicate that SO-WCDs(s) have multiple decay channels and multi-excitation triplet states. The phosphorescence spectra do not show a clear excitation wavelength dependence as the excitation wavelength increases from 340 nm to 420 nm (Fig. 6b).

((a) The phosphorescence excitation and emission spectrum of the SO-WCDs under 365 nm excitation; (b) The phosphorescence emission spectrum of the SO-WCDs at different excitation wavelengths; (c) the phosphorescence decay curve of the SO-WCDs under 365 nm excitation; (d) Fluorescence, phosphorescence emission images of the SO-WCDs at 365 nm)

We also observed the stability of SO-WCDs and P-CDs. We exposed SO-WCDs(s), SO-WCDs(l), and P-CDs samples to UV lamp irradiation for 0, 6 and 12 h and observed their fluorescence changes. The central wavelength and FWHM of the fluorescence peaks of all samples were kept constant. No decrease in fluorescence intensity was seen after 6, and 12 h of exposure, respectively (Fig. S5, Supporting Information). These facts indicate that SO-WCDs and P-CDs have good fluorescence stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The luminescence mechanism of the SO-WCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Why do SO-WCDs exhibit multiple optical properties, such as warm white lighting, cold white lighting, and phosphorescence? Here, we delve into the luminescence mechanism to gain a deeper understanding of the unique optical characteristics of SO-WCDs. We determined mesoscopic light emission sources of our SO-WCDS. General speaking, there are two sources for CDs fluorescence (luminous center) from mesoscopic viewpoint, carbon core and surface state, respectively. To clarify the light emission sources, we studied the wave dependence on solvent polarity and pH, and UV absorption of our SO-WCDs. The solvent dependence of optical properties the SO-WCDs was investigated for illustrating the mesoscopic origin of the SO-WCDs luminescence. Fluorescence spectra of the SO-WCDs in the six solvents (DI-water, DMSO, methanol, acetic acid, ethanol and dichloromethane were used, with gradually decreasing polarity) were measured, respectively (Fig. S6, Supporting Information). With solvent polarity changing, no redshift of emission peaks was observed. The luminescence profiles of SO-WCDs in various solvents were essentially identical, suggesting that solvent polarity does not significantly influence their luminescence. This finding confirms that the intrinsic fluorescence of SO-WCDs primarily stems from their carbon cores, rather than from surface states. Then the impact of pH (protonation effect) on the fluorescence of SO-WCDs was investigated by introducing controlled quantities of hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) into the aqueous solution of SO-WCDs. It was observed that the fluorescence of the SO-WCDs remained unchanged, indicating that the surface state is not the primary origin of their fluorescence, being similar to Refs. [37,38]. The phenomena may be associated with the better stability of the crosslinked polymeric network structure of the SO-WCDs [39], it makes the carbon dots less susceptible to acidic and alkaline environments (Fig. S7, Supporting Information). UV–Vis absorption analysis of the SO-WCDs was conducted. In the analysis, the SO-WCDs exhibited a broad absorption spectrum extending from the UV region to almost the entire visible region, meaning multiple absorption trap existing in SO-WCDs possibly. The small and sharp absorption peak at 240 nm is attributed to the π-π∗ transition of sp2 C species and that of the broad peak (ranging from 300-800, centered at 360 nm) corresponds to the n-π∗ of other sub-fluorophores (such as CO, CN) in carbon cores (Fig. 5d). The fluorescence and phosphorescence excitation spectra of the SO-WCDs(s) match well with the absorption bands at 300 nm–400 nm (Fig. 5a vs. Fig. 6a). It suggests that the solid-state white light and the phosphorescence may arise mainly from the sp2 C and graphitic N contribution of SO-WCDs carbon cores [40–43], namely luminous centers of our SO-WCDs. To further analyze the phosphorescence emission, we recorded the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of SO-WCDs(l) and SO-WCDs(s) at low temperature (77 K) (Figs. S11 and S12, Supporting Information). The phosphorescence exists in both liquid and solid states of carbon dots at low temperatures(77 K), mainly due to the fact that the low-temperature conditions are favorable for the formation and stabilization of triplet excitons, which enhances the phosphorescence emission. At the same time, we can find that the aggregation induction redshift effect cause phosphorescence redshift [44]. The strong H-bonding in aggregated SO-WCD(s) narrow further the bandgap between singlet energy levels and triplet energy levels, as a result, the phosphorescence of the SO-WCD(s) redshift to longer wavelength.

In order to further illustrate the phosphorescence mechanism, we did simple bandgap calculations and theoretical speculations. As the aggregation degree of the SO-WCDs increases, the quantum confinement effect shifts up, energy level splitting occurs, consequently, the aggregation leads to a reduction in the energy level (Es). The Es was red-shifted from 4.96 eV to 1.62 eV (rough estimation from UV–Vis of Fig. S10, Supporting Information), resulting in a gradual red-shift of SO-WCDs from blue fluorescence in the liquid state to yellow fluorescence in the solid state (Fig. 7 , Fig. S10, Supporting Information). The S1 and T1 energy levels of SO-WCDs(s) were obtained by low-temperature fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra. Since the low temperature (77 K) can effectively suppress the non-radiative decay, all the excited energy of SO-WCDs(s) contributes to fluorescence and phosphorescence emission. While the energy level of the fluorescence of the SO-WCDs(s) (ET) = 2.214 eV and the energy level of the phosphorescence of the SO-WCDs(s) (ET) = 2.067 eV at a low temperature of 77k (rough estimation), according to method by Yang et al. [45]. Consequently, the singlet-triplet energy gap, 0.147 eV, was low, which promoted the ISC process and accumulated more triplet excitons to T1, thus achieving phosphoresce. The result is reasonable under the polymeric network nature in the SO-WCDs(s) [46,47]. The strong crosslinked network structure of the SO-WCD(s)s suppresses nonradiative transition and promotes intersystem crossing by suppressing the vibrational degrees of freedom of the SO-WCDs(s). This drives a shift from yellow to orange in SO-WCDs(s) (Fig. 7, Fig. S12, Supporting Information). These strong effects greatly contribute to phosphorescence emission.

At last, we proceed to analyze why the SO-WCDs emit white light, also warm white light in the solid state but cold white light in the liquid state. From the above analysis, we had demonstrated that the SO-WCDs had at least two luminescence centers (sp2 C and sub-fluorophores, respectively, in UV–Vis absorption). Multiple emission, such as multi fluorescence components (three primitive colors or complement color) [48], is a prerequisite for white light. It is evident that the two emissions mentioned above are neither complementary colors nor primary colors. Consequently, the presence of these two luminescence centers alone does not guarantee white emission in the SO-WCDs. The fact had been proved by lots of previous works, for instances, Du et al.’s CDs with the identical UV–Vis absorption (240 nm and 360 nm) only emitted blue fluorescence centered at 457 nm [49], Liu et al.’s CDs with the identical UV–Vis absorption (240 nm and 360 nm) only emitted blue fluorescence centered at 450 nm [50], Xiong et al.’s CDs with the similar UV–Vis absorption (240 nm and 350 nm) only emitted blue fluorescence centered at 450 nm [51]. A question naturally rise why our SO-WCDs with two luminescence can emit white fluorescence? The underlying cause is the aggregation-induced redshift (AIR) of the SO-WCDs and/or phosphorescence. The fluorescence of SO-WCDs exhibited concentration dependence when varying masses of SO-WCDs were added to deionized water. The emission peak of the SO-WCDs was red-shifted with increasing SO-WCDs concentration, from 435 nm to 518 nm, and then to 580 nm in the pure solid state, indicating typical AIR characteristics (Fig. S8, Supporting Information). The observation is followed to the facts that the SO-WCDs hold innately large amount of polymeric crosslinked network structure mentioned above, promoting AIR phenomenon. The AIR fluorescence expands the fluorescence emission bandwidth of the SO-WCDs, and also increase chromaticity spectral component. The AIR fluorescence, together the inherent fluorescence (associated with the peak at 240 nm and 360 nm in UV–Vis absorption) realizes the cold white emission of SO-WCDs(l). For further solidifying our deduction, we explored the fluorescence of some SO-WCDs(l) with various concentration of the SO-WCDs. These SO-WCDs(l) showed various white fluorescence, ranging from blue-white to cool-white to green-white and finally to warm-white in the pure solid state when the concentration increases, and their color temperature also changed with concentration of the SO-WCDs (Fig. S9, Supporting Information). On SO-WCDs(s), we believed that their warm white light should be the jointly contribution of the inherent fluorescence, the AIR fluorescence and the phosphorescence of the SO-WCDs(s). The phosphorescence enrichens the spectral component of the SO-WCDs(s) and thus more approximate to natural light, consequently emitting warm white light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of white light and phosphorescent properties of SO-WCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The SO-WCDs were integrated with UV chip into two WLEDs, the wWLED from the solid state warm white light and the cWLED from the liquid state warm white light. The wWLED is with CIE coordinates, (0.41, 0.43), and lower color temperature, CCT = 3765 K, higher color rendering index (CRI), CRI = 90.6. The cWLED has more near pure whiteness with CIE coordinates (0.29, 0.34), but higher color temperature, CCT = 7495 K, lower CRI, CRI = 78.2(Fig. 8 ). The CDs with CCT tunability are very promising for a great number fields, such as color monitoring and display devices. When we need a more comfortable and natural light environment, the wWLED can be selected and realized only by the solid state of SO-WCDs.

The phosphorescence of SO-WCDs is also applied to visualize LFP for criminal investigation techniques or ID identification (Fig. 9 ). Firstly, we pressed a LFP on a yellow paper (Fig. 9a), then the SO-WCDs powders were scattered on the LFP. The SO-WCDs were excited by UV lamp (365 nm) and the fluorescent LFP were recorded (Fig. 9b). Apparently, the fluorescent LFP record isn't clear, posing difficulties for LFP recording and information identification. Finally, the ultraviolet light was turned off, the phosphorescent LFP were recorded (Fig. 9c). Fig. 9c shows the phosphorescent LFP is clearer. The observation confirms that the phosphorescence of the SO-WCDs is more beneficial to latent trace developing due to the fact that there is no the interference of background fluorescence on the latent trace in phosphorescence developing.

At last, we compared our SO-WCDs with the published white light CDs [7,12,14,27,48,52–69], and we find that our SO-WCDs have significant advantages in precursor reduction(one precursor), industrialization scale-up (solvent-free technique, nearer to large scale production than wet chemistry technique), and warm/cold white light in one formulation (more detailed comparison, see Table S1, support information).

In summary, we successfully synthesized fluorescence–phosphorescence dual emissive, single-component dual state (solid/liquid) white-lighting-emission CDs by one precursor-based solvent-free technique, for the first time. The SO-WCDs held obvious carbon polymeric dot characteristics, and presented multi luminescence, including solid state warm white light, solid state phosphorescence and liquid state cold white light. The SO-WCDs(s) produces warm white light with a low correlated color temperature (CCT = 3765 K) and high color rendering index (CRI = 90.6), a potential candidate for natural light-like WLEDs. Based on optical physics analysis and microscopic characterization, it was concluded that the main luminescence contribution of the SO-WCDs is from carbon core; the cold white light of the CDs is originated from the shared contribution of both the inherent fluorescence and the AIR fluorescence; while the warm white light of the CDs from the shared contribution of the inherent fluorescence, the AIR fluorescence and the phosphoresce. Our study provides a simple way to prepare low-cost, high-performance single-component white light CDs and demonstrates the potential application of SO-WCDs in optoelectronic devices and LFP detection.

Yang Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiaoyong Chen: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jiajie Wu: Supervision, Methodology. Zeyu Zhang: Supervision. Bin Qin: Funding acquisition.
This work was supported by the National Key Laboratory of Transient Shock. Project fund number 6142606222106
